data.title: OMA ratifies External Auditor for the Company and its subsidiaries
data.description:
Mexico City, Mexico, August 8, 2025— Grupo Aeroportuario del Centro Norte, S.A.B. de C.V. (BMV: OMA; NASDAQ: OMAB), informs that its Board of Directors, based on the recommendation of its Audit Committee, has ratified the firm Galaz, Yamazaki, Ruiz Urquiza, S.C., member of Deloitte Touche Tohmatsu Limited, to provide external audit services for the Company and its subsidiaries for the year ending December 31, 2025.

About OMA

Grupo Aeroportuario del Centro Norte, S.A.B. de C.V., known as OMA, operates 13 international airports in nine states of central and northern Mexico. OMA’s airports serve Monterrey, Mexico’s third largest metropolitan area, the tourist destinations of Acapulco, Mazatlán, and Zihuatanejo, and nine other regional centers and border cities. OMA also operates the NH Collection Hotel inside Terminal 2 of the Mexico City airport and the Hilton Garden Inn at the Monterrey airport. OMA employs over 1,200 persons in order to offer passengers and clients airport and commercial services in facilities. OMA is listed on the Mexican Stock Exchange (OMA) and on the NASDAQ Global Select Market (OMAB). Since December 2022, OMA is part of VINCI Airports, the world´s leading private airport operator. data.title: Wheat Futures Fall on Global Oversupply
data.description:
Wheat futures closed lower around $5.15 per bushel on Friday, due to an oversupply in the global market.

Large harvests across the Northern Hemisphere have flooded the market with new-crop wheat, weighing on prices.

While lower prices have stimulated demand—reflected in stronger-than-expected U.S. export sales—the overwhelming supply continues to dominate market sentiment.

Russia, the world’s largest wheat exporter, raised its 2025 crop forecast to 84.5 million metric tons, heightening concerns over global surplus.

Meanwhile, increased wheat planting and favorable early growing conditions in Argentina have added further to the abundant supply outlook.

data.title: Nvidia Stock Nears New Record. 2 Reasons for the Rally. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Clark and Anita Hamilton

Nvidia stock is on track to close at another all-time high Friday, which would be its 19th this year. A new artificial-intelligence model from OpenAI and the potential for increased spending from Tesla look set to keep the rally in shares going.

Nvidia shares were up 1.13% at $182.82 Friday afternoon with less than an hour left in the regular trading day. The stock closed at $180.77 yesterday, its 18th record close in 2025. It's up 36% year to date and nearly 75% over the last 52 weeks, with a market capitalization of $4.41 trillion.

The company got a boost this week despite President Donald Trump's proposed 100% tariff on chip imports. That's because there appear to be widespread exemptions for companies like Nvidia that make semiconductors in the U.S. Nvidia also announced in April that it plans to produce up to $500 billion in AI infrastructure in the U.S. over the next four years.

There appear to be few obstacles to strong investment in AI technology. OpenAI launched GPT-5 on Thursday, the latest model to power the popular ChatGPT. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman named Nvidia as one of the key partners for the technology, and it was the only pure-play chip company mentioned.

Nvidia could also be set to receive more cash from Tesla, which is slimming down its internal chip lineup. The electric-vehicle maker is disbanding its Dojo supercomputing team and associated in-house AI training chips, and will rely more on hardware from Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

"It doesn't make sense for Tesla to divide its resources and scale two quite different AI chip designs," Tesla CEO Elon Musk said in a post on social-media site X late on Thursday.

Musk said Tesla's focus will be on its AI5 and AI6 chips that specialize in inference — producing output from AI models — rather than training, where Nvidia's hardware is dominant. Tesla didn't immediately respond to a request for comment early on Friday.

Among other chip makers, Advanced Micro Devices was down 0.16% and Broadcom was up 0.4% in late afternoon trading.

Write to Adam Clark at adam.clark@barrons.com and Anita Hamilton at anita.hamilton@barrons.com

data.title: Datadog Is Maintained at Sector Outperform by Scotiabank
ICE Review: Canola Drops to End Week
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--The ICE Futures canola market was weaker on Friday, taking back Thursday's gains as losses in Chicago soyoil and improved moisture conditions in parts of Western Canada weighed on values.

Chart-based speculative selling was a feature, with the November contract settling below its 100-day moving average.

European rapeseed was also lower, while Malaysian palm oil and the Canadian dollar held relatively steady.

Tight old crop supplies and solid end user demand remained supportive underneath the market.

There were 32,074 contracts traded on Friday, which compares with Thursday, when 47,389 contracts changed hands.

Spreading accounted for 16,974 of the contracts traded.

data.title: An 'Impressive' Outfit for a Regular Guy? Cue a Weekend-Long Shopping Expedition — WSJ
data.description:
By Jamie Waters

My partner, Cam, recently announced that he had no idea what to wear to his friends' overseas wedding. Could I help? You bet! I love telling him what to wear. Confident he could manage black-tie on his own, I assumed this must be a cocktail affair.

Wrong. He pointed to a three-word riddle at the top of the invitation: "Dress to impress!"

Dress to impress? I've found clearer directives in fortune cookies. When I demanded more intel, Cam mentioned that the bride's dress was a custom creation by a world-famous costume designer (a family friend). Oh, and it was bright pink. Clearly a corporate suit would be as welcome as a sloshed uncle slurring his way through an impromptu toast.

Cryptic dress codes for weddings and other social events are trending, according to Patrick Kenger, a personal stylist in Southern California. He blames this on hosts wanting to encourage creativity. (The vaguest theme he has encountered lately was "Upscale California," which sounds like a Goop candle.)

We resolved to treat this as an opportunity. Cam, a millennial with decent style and a regular office job, had mentioned that his closet felt tired. Here was a chance for some sartorial caffeination.

He wanted to look "hot, cool — and definitely not forced." But the items would also need to perform in settings beyond a Chardonnay-soaked dance floor. He floated $500-$800 as a ballpark budget for a full outfit.

I told him to block out a weekend for a Manhattan shopping expedition — and to limber up, because there would be no dawdling.

We set out one scorching Saturday — ideal conditions for rushing about and chucking on jackets. I figured a super-casual suit could anchor his outfit. Chill two-pieces in cloths like cotton-twill and linen line the racks at trendy brands. Some cost a fraction of classic tailoring.

At Todd Snyder , our first stop, I realized I had misjudged our dynamic. While inspecting buttons, I overheard Cam tell the clerk, "Oh don't worry, my partner will bring things to me." I promptly delivered a $348 cream linen jacket that His Highness deemed "too cultlike." (I was searching for the matching pants when he made his pronouncement).

As we blazed through Officine Générale , Polo Ralph Lauren and Knickerbocker I insisted he try double-breasted jackets. Not just for King Charles, the once-snooty style is being destuffed via casual cuts — and worn open, like a cardigan. "They can be rakishly sexy," I explained. "I look like an olive that's exploded," he announced after shrugging on a flappy, green, chino-cotton jacket. He was equally stumped by the pleated-trouser trend. After trying three pairs that bagged around the crotch, he accused me of putting him in diapers.

Buck Mason reminded us that simplicity rules. The $346 Carry-On suit — unlined single-breasted jacket, flat-front pants — won points for its throw-it-on attitude. In a slightly faded navy cotton-blend, it brought to mind a French chore jacket and chinos that had gotten spiffy for dinner. And that price? Criminal. Into the bag it went.

Over iced lattes, Cam, forgetting that I was project manager, announced that he wouldn't need new shoes because his sneakers would go perfectly. I agreed they would be superb...if this were 2015. Today, minimalist white leather sneakers and a dark suit just looks dated. When I suggested loafers, he scrunched his nose: He finds them "a bit poncy, like precious slippers." That's a common concern, said the stylist Kenger. But, he added, once skeptics experience the comfort of loafers, they're usually sold. He proposed brown penny loafers in suede or pebbled leather to complement the suit's matteness.

We ducked into Meermin, a great-value brand. When I saw prospective customers pacing about in gym shorts and slightly pointy loafers, like sporty Little Lord Fauntleroys, I knew this place would not convince Cam. A clerk called the $200-ish loafers "sleek and slim." Cam christened them "needle shoes." In any case, I had stopped caring about shoes, for I was wrestling with a graver problem. The clerk had handed Cam the world's thinnest "no-show" socks (to try with loafers), and seeing your partner in what are basically sheer ballet slippers is a sight that cannot be unseen.

It was time for the chunkiest loafers in town. At Blackstock & Weber , whose lug-sole kicks I once heard described as "the four-wheel drive of loafers," the clerk plucked a chocolate tassel loafer from the display. It was the last one — and Cam's size. Like Cinderella in a turbocharged slipper, he strutted about...and liked what he saw. His verdict: "streamlined but not flimsy — and just a little flashy." Above all, he loved their padded comfort. As this was a sample pair, the pebble-grain, Goodyear-welted beauties cost $370 instead of $425. Cam was now a loafer guy.

Blackstock's clerk gave us a neat tip: Wear socks one shade darker than your loafers for a cohesive combo. Cue $5 Uniqlo dress-socks the color of bitter Lindt. A $90 brass-buckled Le Alfré belt that matched the loafers' grainy hide also hit the cart.

We needed a superlative shirt to complete the look. Kenger emphasized that a non-shiny cloth like Oxford would keep the outfit's textures and casualness consistent. Mitchell Belk, a British stylist, suggested linen shirts with thick lemon or pink butcher stripes. Cam spent half of Sunday buttoning up striped designs at J.Crew , Uniqlo and multibrand store Cueva . Lots were nice, and could be livened up with a tie. But they felt too polite for a pink-gowned bride.

Figuring a suit as chill as Cam's could tango with a cheeky shirt, we changed tack. When we told the clerk at a fashionable downtown store that we wanted "a fun shirt," he nodded. "Say no more," he said, returning with a knit polo in a beige so beige it made khaki look racy. Were we being pranked?

We were determined to escape bland land. We went too far with Polo Ralph Lauren's $195 vintage polo covered in what looked like sardines. Tombolo's $148 crocodile-print shirt also risked turning Cam into That Wedding Guest.

Safe or kitsch seemed the only options until we spotted the short-sleeve Leisure Shirt at 3sixteen . The $240 cream cotton style boasted textured stripes and a large, splayed collar inspired by 1950s Italian spread collars. That raffish collar sat neatly under a jacket — and would shine on the dance floor when unleashed. In the changing room, Cam slung his jacket over his shoulder and did a spin. He looked like a classy extra on "Miami Vice."

The in-store sizes didn't fit perfectly, but he found his size online, and on sale, for $205. With the upper limit of his budget in the rearview mirror, we huddled for an inspired round of justifications. All you need to know is that he got the Leisure Shirt — and that he'll be living in it for the next five summers.

data.title: Idea Tech Holding Limited Files for IPO
data.description:
(18:49 GMT) *Idea Tech Holding Files for 2M Share IPO
(18:49 GMT) *Idea Tech Holding Expects IPO to Price Between $4 and $5
(18:49 GMT) *Idea Tech Holding: Applied to List on the Nasdaq Capital Market Under the Symbol IDTL
(19:21 GMT) Idea Tech Holding Limited Files for IPO

By Natalie Weger

Cayman Islands holding company Idea Tech Holding has filed for an initial public offering.

The company plans to offer 2 million units between $4 to $5 apiece, according to a Friday filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

Idea Tech Holding plans to list shares on the Nasdaq Capital Market under the ticker IDTL.

The company has granted the representative of the underwriters, R.F. Lafferty & Co., a 45-day option to purchase up to 15% of the total number of ordinary shares from the company.

Idea Tech Holding said it is a provider of STEM and technological innovation education solutions. The company conducts all operations in Hong Kong through the operating subsidiary, Ask Idea.

Write to Natalie Weger at natalie.weger@wsj.com

data.title: ICE canola futures fall Friday and for week
data.description:
ICE canola futures fell on Friday and for the holiday-shortened week, dragged lower by weakness in Chicago soyoil.

• November canola (RSX5) settled down $7.90 at $669.40 per metric ton. January (RSF6) fell $9.10 to $681.

• Canola futures had only one day of gains during the week but stayed within a range that some traders are describing as summer doldrums, with little news to move the market. The market was closed on Monday.

• Canola is following soyoil for general price direction, traders said.

• Canadian canola crops have been helped by rainfall in many areas this week, which will help crops reach their full potential. Some analysts are boosting their production estimates due to the good weather since early July in most areas.

• Chicago soyoil (BOv1) and canola are mostly divorced from the other crop markets now that biofuels demand makes up so much of the value of vegetable oils in North America. "That's the power of government policy," said analyst and broker David Derwin of Ventum Financial about biofuels demand and vegoils.

• Chicago Board of Trade soybean futures , corn and wheat have fallen to near multi-year lows, while soyoil futures (BOv1) remains near 21 month highs. Canola has fallen further than soyoil in the recent weakness, but is still relatively strong compared to other crops.

• Euronext August rapeseed futures (COMc1) fell 1.16% and fell from last week. Rapeseed has been in a declining trend since November.

• Malaysian palm oil futures rose 0.40% on Friday and rose for the week.

• The Canadian dollar had a small gain for the week.

data.title: Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman, LLC Encourages Marex Group plc (MRX) Investors to Inquire about Securities Investigation
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / August 8, 2025 / Bronstein, Gewirtz & Grossman, LLC is investigating potential claims on behalf of purchasers of Marex Group plc ("Marex" or "the Company") . Investors who purchased Marex securities are encouraged to obtain additional information and assist the investigation by visiting the firm's site: bgandg.com/MRX.

Investigation Details

On August 5, 2025, during market hours, NINGI Research published a report entitled "Marex Group plc: A Financial House of Cards." In announcing the report, NINGI Research stated that, in its opinion, "Marex has engaged in a multi-year accounting scheme involving a web of opaque off-balance-sheet entities, fictitious intercompany transactions, and misleading disclosures to conceal significant losses, inflate profits, and mask its true risk exposure. We have uncovered evidence suggesting Marex is a financial house of cards, with a balance sheet riddled with holes and financials that we believe are unreliable." Following this news, Marex stock fell $2.92 per share, or 7.6%, to close at $35.31 on August 5, 2025.

data.title: Tesla, Palantir, Alphabet Surge as AI Buzz Lifts Tech Giants
data.description:
Aug 8 - Tesla (TSLA, Financial) and Palantir Technologies led mega-cap gains on Friday as broader markets advanced, buoyed by AI developments and investor positioning around Federal Reserve leadership shifts, according to a Friday press release.

Palantir rose nearly 3% after Unstructured joined its FedStart program, designed to help software firms meet federal compliance faster. Tesla added more than 3% amid ongoing AI sector strength. Alphabet also gained over 2%.

Large-cap stocks saw sharp moves as well. Gilead Sciences jumped 8% following a Buy rating upgrade from Truist Securities on HIV treatment momentum, while Natera surged 14% on a revenue beat. Doximity (DOCS), GenOn Energy (GEN), and Sotera Health (SHC) rose between 11% to 20%.

On the downside, The Trade Desk plunged 38% after a Sell rating from MoffettNathanson on valuation concerns. Twilio sank 17%, while Under Armour , Sweetgreen , and Sezzle all posted double-digit losses.

data.title: Soybean Futures Fall as Analysts Mull WASDE Possibilities — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Soybeans for November delivery fell 0.7% to $9.86 1/4 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade on Friday, with Tuesday's WASDE report from the USDA potentially showing a significant lift in yield projections.
  • Wheat for September delivery fell 0.5% to $5.15 3/4 a bushel.
  • Corn for December delivery fell 0.4% to $4.05 1/2 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Trending Higher: Grain traders spent the day looking ahead to next week's WASDE report.

Yields and production are expected to rise from the previous month, with analysts debating how much they climb.

Soybean yields may show the biggest uptick, said Karen Braun of Zaner Ag Hedge.

"If I had to vote for a potential yield surprise scenario this year, it would be soybean yield to the high side," she said.

She points out that the USDA has raised its soybean yield forecast in its August report from the prior month in eight of the past 10 years, with the largest of those upticks being an increase of 7% in 2020.

Passing Through: A cold front passing through the Corn Belt this weekend will serve to keep the weather favorable for growing row crops over the weekend, the USDA says.

"A cold front crossing the Plains and Midwest, as well as a high-pressure system over the Northeast, will help to cap the northward extent of heat expanding from the Southwest," the USDA said. "Consequently, Midwestern temperatures should remain mostly favorable for summer crops, including corn and soybeans, for the next several days." Rainfall is also expected for these areas.

INSIGHT

Crop Cut: Black Sea consultancy SovEcon has cut its projection for the Russian wheat crop, reducing it by 300,000 metric tons to 83.3 million tons.

While it is a reduction in crop size, it is still up 700,000 tons from Russia's production last year, according to the firm.

A degradation of crop conditions in the southern part of the country has impacted the firm's outlook, SovEcon said in a note Friday, with lower yields cutting production in the south by 900,000 tons.

"As of late August, harvesting in the south is nearing completion, making a recovery in yields unlikely," said SovEcon.

South American Share: Chinese data are showing that the country imported 11.7 million metric tons of soybeans in July, up 18% from this time last year.

Overall, 61 million tons of soybeans have been imported in the first seven months of the year, up 4.6% from the same timeframe last year.

While data for the origins of the soybeans aren't yet available for July, in June 87% of the soybeans imported by China came from Brazil.

With the current trade climate, U.S. sales may stay under wraps through the rest of the year, said Commerzbank in a note.

Spare No Expense: World food prices rose last month, driven by meat and vegetable oils despite declines in cereal, dairy and sugar prices, the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations said.

The FAO's food price index, which tracks global prices for a basket of staple foods, averaged 130.1 points in July, a 1.6% increase from June and 7.6% higher than a year earlier. The index remains 18.8% below its peak reached in March 2022 after Russia's invasion of Ukraine.

Meat prices rose 1.2% to a record high, supported by strong U.S. and Chinese import demand for bovine and ovine meats. Vegetable-oil prices surged 7.1% from the previous month on palm, soy and sunflower oils, reaching a three-year high.

AHEAD

  • The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly grain export inspections report at 11 a.m. EDT Monday.
  • The USDA is due to release its weekly crop progress report at 4 p.m. EDT Monday.
  • Smithfield Foods Inc. is scheduled to release its 2Q earnings before the stock market opens Tuesday.
  • The USDA is due to release its monthly WASDE report at noon EDT Tuesday.
  • Giulia Petroni contributed to this article.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: This Pizza Chain's Stock Surged 360%--But Is the Slice Still Worth the Price?
data.description:
Once a turnaround story, Casey's has quietly become one of the market's most impressive retail compounders. Since 2018, shares have climbed more than 360% as the company revamped its strategy under CEO Darren Rebelezcentralizing procurement, streamlining operations, and betting big on fresh food. That bet's paid off. Prepared food now contributes nearly a third of gross profit, well above peers, while private-label items are benefiting from the company's strong reputation. Bloomberg Second Measure data shows Casey's outpacing the broader staples sector in sales growthdriven by pizza, donuts, breakfast burritos, and even a trial menu of wings and fries.

But it's not just food that's fueling the run. Casey's has leaned into M&A, targeting small chains that make up over 60% of the 150,000+ U.S. convenience stores. Its biggest deal yetlast year's $1.1 billion acquisition of CEFCOadded nearly 200 stores and a key fuel terminal, extending Casey's reach into high-growth states like Texas and Florida. The company has been adding around 100 stores a year, with a current footprint of about 2,900 locations across 19 states. That's still far behind giants like 7-Eleven or Circle K, but Casey's rural focus gives it pricing power and customer loyalty in markets with limited competition.

Still, not every analyst is on board at these levels. The stock closed at $518.28 on Thursday, trading at roughly 35x earningsa premium to convenience store peers and even most pizza chains. BMO's Kelly Bania noted that rising expectations leave little margin for error, while Wells Fargo's Anthony Bonadio flagged that any slowdown in growthor signs of strength from other retailerscould trigger a rotation. Others remain constructive, pointing to Casey's unique traffic drivers: fuel, beer, tobacco, and foodan edge that traditional QSRs can't match. But with the bar set this high, Casey's next chapter will need more than just good pizza.

data.title: During Lazy, Hazy Summer Days, Advisors Find New Ways to Connect With Clients — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Sally Cates

Summer may signal a slowdown for some industries, but for many financial advisors, it simply means adjusting the rhythm. Whether that entails hopping on planes to meet clients at their second homes, cruising along the coast of Maine with lobster rolls in tow, or staying digitally connected from afar, top advisors find creative ways to maintain relationships without disrupting the season's slower, more relaxed pace.

We asked three advisors how they are navigating client communications, referrals, and relationship-building during the summer months. Their insights show that meaningful engagement doesn't take a vacation.

Elizabeth (Lisa) van Walleghem, CEO and founder, Maximai Investment Partners, Coral Gables Fla.

We're based in Coral Gables, Fla., and for our U.S. clients, we do travel to their vacation homes, as I did in early June. We also visit them in their primary residences from time to time. And we conduct many zoom meetings with clients that are in states other than Florida or are outside the U.S. Our goal is to visit our clients personally at least two times a year.

We haven't really adjusted our frequency of communication for summer, it's business as usual. We know when our clients are on vacation and respect their time by not bothering them unless it's something urgent.

Since things slow down just a bit during the summer months, that gives us time to focus on our interns. Our goal is to create future advocates.

We also focus on making connections and prospecting on LinkedIn. I am always open to meeting new people and attracting opportunities that turn into new business. Over the summer I enjoy all the gatherings, vacations, and parties that clients and friends host. There I often meet new people who become clients over time.

Helen Andreoli, managing partner, Great Diamond Partners, Portland, Maine

Our beautiful state of Maine is called "Vacationland" because it's such an ideal place to visit and in the summer, clients flock to Maine. The most successful event we ever did, which clients still talk about, was a sunset cruise around Casco Bay during which we provided some lovely beverages and, most importantly, a big pile of lobster rolls!

One creative thing we did for a client was provide beach towels with the names of all 12 of her grandchildren. When they visit for the summer, they have their own special towel to take down to the beach. For the client, I believe she saw from that gesture that we are paying attention, not just to her finances, but to her family relationships, which are the most important thing to her.

More generally, we've found that clients, particularly in Maine, want to enjoy the summer with their families so we tend to have client review meetings more regularly in the spring and fall. Our communication strategies (newsletters, market commentary, and check-in calls) don't change at all in the summer months.

In the winter, our clients generally head south to escape the cold. During those frigid months is when we do our client travel visiting many clients, mostly in Florida. In person meetings are important as there is so much to be gained from sitting together in a family's living room or around a dining table.

Matt Cavanaugh, managing partner and director of investments, XXI Wealth, Chicago, Ill.

Every client is a little different. We try to be respectful of important family and vacation time and adapt to each client's needs and communication styles.

For some clients, summer is a time to unplug and relax. For others, such as some professional golfers and baseball players, it is right in the height of their busy season.

Every client is unique, and we are thoughtful to adapt to what works best for them. In that sense, in summer, it is business as usual.

Sally Cates is a managing director and is responsible for public relations and communications for Dynasty Financial Partners and the Dynasty Network of Advisors. Previously, she led global communications at Citi Private Bank and Smith Barney.
